where-am-i.label: Where Am I? pwd and ls
where-am-i.summary: Use pwd and ls to inspect your current folder and see what is inside it.

sketch0.title: pwd tells you your current location

sketch2.title: Relative paths start from where you are

try_where_am_i_sketch0.title: Check your current folder
try_where_am_i_sketch0.prompt: Run `pwd` and look for the workspace path in the terminal output.

try_where_am_i_sketch1.title: List the current folder
try_where_am_i_sketch1.prompt: Run `ls` and make sure `README.md` and `terminal-map` appear in the terminal output.

try_where_am_i_sketch2.title: Show hidden files too
try_where_am_i_sketch2.prompt: Run `ls -a` so hidden files appear too, then look for `.hidden-map`.

ci-create-navigation-lab.title: Check your current folder
ci-create-navigation-lab.prompt:
Run `pwd` and look for the workspace path in the terminal output.

Use the terminal. The editor is hidden on purpose for this task.
ci-create-navigation-lab.hint: Use `pwd` to print the current working directory.

help.concept: This task is checked from your terminal command and terminal output.
help.hint_1: Run `pwd` exactly once or a few times until you see the current folder.
help.hint_2: Check Answer looks for the `pwd` command and for `/workspace` in the printed path.

pwd-current-place.title: pwd tells you your current location
pwd-current-place.bodyMarkdown:
`pwd` means **print working directory**. It shows the folder your terminal is currently inside.

```bash
pwd
```

When a task says “create a file here,” your current directory matters. Running `pwd` before making changes is a good habit.

ls-shows-nearby-files.title: ls lists what is nearby
ls-shows-nearby-files.bodyMarkdown:
`ls` shows files and folders in the current directory.

```bash
ls
ls -a
ls -l
```

`ls -a` includes hidden files. `ls -l` shows a longer listing with extra details. In Course 1, use `ls` often to check your work before clicking Check Answer.

paths-start-from-here.title: Relative paths start from where you are
paths-start-from-here.bodyMarkdown:
A relative path like `notes/today.txt` starts from your current directory.

Example:

```bash
mkdir -p navigation-lab/notes
touch navigation-lab/notes/today.txt
```

This creates folders and a file under the current workspace folder.
